course.summary: Master the architecture, technology choices, and engineering tradeoffs behind the Meridian full-stack AI workspace — including where alternatives exist and why each decision was made.

modules.title: TypeScript Strict Mode: The Stack's Foundation
modules.strata_theme: Theme 1 — TypeScript strict mode as the universal prerequisite
modules.description: Why strict mode is not a preference but a technical contract, and how every library in the Meridian stack depends on it.
objectives: Configure TypeScript strict mode and explain what each of the nine sub-flags does
Identify which Meridian libraries silently degrade without strict mode and why

lessons.title: The Nine Flags: What strict:true Actually Enables
lessons.description: The nine strict sub-flags, which ones matter most for the Meridian stack, and how isolatedModules interacts with Vite.

lessons.title: Strict Mode Through Libraries: Zod, Zustand, and the Anthropic SDK
lessons.description: How z.infer<>, Drizzle table types, and the Anthropic SDK's MessageParam types each depend on strict mode to work correctly.

modules.title: Immutability: The Architectural Principle Behind Three Layers
modules.strata_theme: Theme 2 — Immutability as the architectural foundation
modules.description: How the same principle — immutable values, copy-on-write mutations — governs React 18's concurrent renderer, Lexical's EditorState, and Yjs's CRDT operations.
objectives: Explain why immutable state is required for React 18's concurrent rendering to be safe
Describe how Lexical's frozen EditorState and Yjs's operation model both derive from the same immutability principle

lessons.title: React 18 Concurrent Rendering and Why Mutation Breaks It
lessons.description: How startTransition, interrupted renders, and StrictMode double-invocation all require immutable state snapshots to be safe.

lessons.title: Immutable Operations in Yjs: The Math Behind CRDT Convergence
lessons.description: Why CRDT commutativity requires each operation to be an immutable value, and how this connects to Lexical's frozen EditorState.

modules.title: The Control-vs-Abstraction Lens
modules.strata_theme: Theme 4 — The control vs abstraction tradeoff as the meta-theme
modules.description: The single decision framework that explains every major technology choice in Meridian: control (direct access to the mechanism) vs abstraction (DX at the cost of opacity).
objectives: Apply the control-vs-abstraction tradeoff to evaluate a technology choice given a set of domain constraints
Identify the specific mechanism that an abstracted alternative would hide for each Meridian component

lessons.title: One Lens for Every Technology Decision
lessons.description: Drizzle vs Prisma, Express vs Fastify, Lexical vs ProseMirror, direct Anthropic SDK vs LangChain — each resolved through one consistent tradeoff question.

modules.title: Single Source of Truth: Schema-as-Type Across the Stack
modules.strata_theme: Theme 3 — Single source of truth, define once, derive everywhere
modules.description: How Zod schemas, Drizzle table definitions, and PostgreSQL RLS policies each express the 'define once, derive everywhere' principle.
objectives: Use drizzle-zod createInsertSchema() to derive a Zod validator from a Drizzle table and use it in an Express route
Implement a PostgreSQL table with RLS and the SET LOCAL pattern so the schema enforces tenant isolation automatically

lessons.title: Schema-as-Type: z.infer<> + drizzle-zod in Practice
lessons.description: The full pattern: Drizzle table definition → drizzle-zod createInsertSchema() → Zod validator in Express route → zero manual type declarations.

lessons.title: PostgreSQL RLS + pgvector: One Table Definition, Three Jobs
lessons.description: How a single Drizzle table definition defines RLS-protected relational columns and a pgvector HNSW-indexed embedding column simultaneously.

modules.title: React 18 + Zustand: The Client State Integration Contract
modules.strata_theme: Theme 5 — React 18 / useSyncExternalStore as the state integration contract
modules.description: How useSyncExternalStore connects React 18's concurrent renderer to external state systems, and why Zustand, Lexical, and AI streaming all honour this contract.
objectives: Implement a Zustand slice that correctly subscribes to an SSE stream in React 18 concurrent mode without tearing
Explain why Vite SPA is architecturally correct for Meridian while Next.js App Router is not

lessons.title: useSyncExternalStore: The Hook That Connects React 18 to the World
lessons.description: What the hook does, why Zustand v4 switched to it, and the pattern for connecting an SSE stream to React state without concurrent-mode tearing.

lessons.title: Vite SPA vs Next.js: Why SSR-First Frameworks Break Meridian
lessons.description: Why Lexical cannot run on the server, why Yjs providers are client-side, and the specific Next.js App Router features that are incompatible with Meridian's real-time architecture.

modules.title: Collaborative Editing: Yjs, CRDTs, and Lexical
modules.strata_theme: Theme 7 — Collaborative editing as a foundational architectural constraint
modules.description: How the CRDT algorithm choice determines the editor choice, and how @lexical/yjs wires an immutable CRDT to an immutable editor state.
objectives: Implement a collaborative Lexical editor with @lexical/yjs and a y-websocket provider
Explain why Yjs's CRDT model enables offline editing while ProseMirror's OT model cannot

lessons.title: CRDTs vs OT: Why the Algorithm Choice Determines the Editor Choice
lessons.description: CRDT commutativity vs OT central authority, and the direct chain: offline requirement → Yjs (CRDT) → Lexical (@lexical/yjs first-class) → not ProseMirror.

lessons.title: @lexical/yjs in Practice: Editor State as a Y.XmlFragment
lessons.description: Wiring a Lexical editor to a Y.Doc via @lexical/yjs: provider setup, awareness (cursor presence), and the offline-sync lifecycle.

modules.title: RAG as AI Memory Architecture
modules.strata_theme: Theme 8 — RAG as the AI memory architecture
modules.description: The four-stage RAG pipeline mapped to Meridian's stack: pgvector for retrieval, Anthropic SDK for generation, and why direct SDK beats abstraction layers.
objectives: Implement the four RAG stages using pgvector's HNSW index and the Anthropic SDK's streaming messages API
Explain why the direct Anthropic SDK is the correct choice given a single-provider architecture

lessons.title: The Four RAG Stages Mapped to the Meridian Stack
lessons.description: Ingestion (chunk → embed → pgvector INSERT), Retrieval (embed query → ORDER BY embedding <=> $1), Augmentation (inject into system prompt), Generation (client.messages.stream()).

lessons.title: Direct Anthropic SDK: Streaming, Tool Calling, and When Not to Abstract
lessons.description: What the Anthropic SDK exposes that Vercel AI SDK and LangChain hide, and the conditions under which abstraction would be the right choice instead.

modules.title: Security: Four Independent Layers
modules.description: How Zod, JWT, bcrypt, and PostgreSQL RLS form four independent layers of defense, each covering a distinct attack surface.
objectives: Implement the four-layer security chain in correct dependency order: Zod → JWT middleware → SET LOCAL → RLS
Identify the specific JWT attacks from RFC 8725 and the Express middleware pattern that prevents each

lessons.title: Four-Layer Security: Zod → JWT → bcrypt → RLS in Correct Order
lessons.description: The full request lifecycle: Zod validates body → JWT middleware extracts workspace_id → SET LOCAL sets RLS context → query executes under RLS. Why order matters.

lessons.title: bcrypt vs Argon2id and the JWT Attacks That Actually Happen
lessons.description: The alg:none and RSA-to-HMAC confusion attacks from RFC 8725, the bcrypt vs Argon2id tradeoff, and when to upgrade Meridian's credential hashing.

modules.title: Monorepo Tooling: Three Scopes, One Project
modules.strata_theme: Theme 9 — Monorepo layers are complementary, not competing
modules.description: How pnpm workspaces, Turborepo, and Docker Compose each operate at distinct scopes and why understanding those scopes prevents tooling confusion.
objectives: Configure a pnpm workspace with workspace:* linking and scoped --filter commands
Identify the observable signal that triggers adding Turborepo to a pnpm workspace

lessons.title: pnpm + Docker Compose + Turborepo: Three Scopes, Not Three Options
lessons.description: Package registry (pnpm workspace:*), task graph (turbo.json pipelines), service runtime (compose.yaml health checks) — each tool's scope, when to add Turborepo, and the Docker Compose health check pattern.

# Strict Mode Through Libraries: Zod, Zustand, and the Anthropic SDK

**Module**: M01 · TypeScript Strict Mode: The Stack's Foundation
**Type**: applied
**Estimated time**: 15 minutes
**Claim**: C2 — TypeScript strict mode's value is realized through library integration

---

## The core idea

Strict mode doesn't just make your own code safer — it's the precondition for three core Meridian libraries to give you accurate types. Zod's schema-to-type derivation, Zustand's store type inference, and the Anthropic SDK's event types all depend on strict mode being active. Without it, the types compile but lie.

## Why it matters

You're building with a stack where a Zod schema IS a TypeScript type — not a source for generating one, not a runtime validator that happens to have a type, but the authoritative definition. If that derivation is wrong, you get false confidence at every call site that uses `z.infer<typeof schema>`. For production code, silent type drift is more dangerous than a compile error.

## A concrete example

**Zod — schema as type**

```typescript
import { z } from "zod"

const CreateWorkspaceSchema = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(1).max(100),
  slug: z.string().regex(/^[a-z0-9-]+$/),
})

// z.infer<> derives the TypeScript type from the schema at compile time
type CreateWorkspaceInput = z.infer<typeof CreateWorkspaceSchema>
// Result (with strict: true):
// { name: string; slug: string }
```

Without `strict: true`, the `z.infer<>` utility can return `any` for certain schema shapes — specifically when conditional types are involved in Zod's internals. You won't get an error. You'll get `any`, which accepts everything. [S010](https://zod.dev/)

**Zustand — curried creation for generic inference**

```typescript
import { create } from "zustand"

interface WorkspaceState {
  workspaceId: string | null
  setWorkspaceId: (id: string) => void
}

// The curried pattern: create<State>()(() => ...) 
// Without strict mode, TypeScript can't infer State correctly
// from the callback alone — the extra () forces the generic to resolve
const useWorkspaceStore = create<WorkspaceState>()((set) => ({
  workspaceId: null,
  setWorkspaceId: (id) => set({ workspaceId: id }),
}))
```

The `create<State>()(() => ...)` double-function pattern looks redundant. It exists specifically because TypeScript's generic inference requires strict mode to correctly resolve the state type from both the generic parameter and the callback simultaneously. Without it, `set` becomes `any`. [S009](https://github.com/pmndrs/zustand/blob/main/README.md)

**Drizzle — table definition as type**

```typescript
import { pgTable, uuid, text, vector } from "drizzle-orm/pg-core"

export const memories = pgTable("memories", {
  id: uuid("id").defaultRandom().primaryKey(),
  workspaceId: uuid("workspace_id").notNull(),
  content: text("content").notNull(),
  embedding: vector("embedding", { dimensions: 1536 }),
})

// Drizzle infers the select/insert types directly from this definition
// typeof memories.$inferSelect → { id: string; workspaceId: string; content: string; embedding: number[] | null }
// No codegen. No .prisma file. The table IS the type.
```

Drizzle's structural type inference depends on `noImplicitAny` to resolve column types accurately. Without it, certain column type helpers return `any` instead of the specific column type. [S005](https://orm.drizzle.team/docs/overview)

## Key points

- `z.infer<typeof schema>` silently returns `any` for some schema shapes without `strict: true` — no error, just wrong types
- Zustand's `create<State>()()` double-function pattern is a deliberate workaround for TypeScript generic inference, requiring strict mode to work correctly
- Drizzle table definitions derive query result types structurally — `strict: true` is what makes those derived types accurate rather than `any`

## Go deeper

- [S010](https://zod.dev/) — Zod docs: `z.infer<>` and strict mode requirements
- [S005](https://orm.drizzle.team/docs/overview) — Drizzle ORM: structural type inference from table definitions
- [S009](https://github.com/pmndrs/zustand/blob/main/README.md) — Zustand: the curried create pattern and why it exists

---

*[← Previous: The Nine Flags](./L01-strict-mode-nine-flags.md)* · *[Next: React 18 Concurrent Rendering →](./L03-react18-concurrent-rendering.md)*
lessons.L03:
# React 18 Concurrent Rendering and Why Mutation Breaks It

**Module**: M02 · Immutability: The Architectural Principle Behind Three Layers
**Type**: core
**Estimated time**: 15 minutes
**Claim**: C4 — React 18's concurrent rendering model mandates immutable state across the entire client stack

---

## The core idea

React 18's concurrent renderer can interrupt a render mid-flight, discard its partial output, and restart later. This is what makes `startTransition` and Suspense safe. But it only works if state is immutable — if React can hold a reference to a past state snapshot and trust that it will never change underneath it. Mutable state turns concurrent rendering from a feature into a source of corrupting bugs.

## Why it matters

Every state-adjacent tool that integrates with Meridian's client — Zustand, Lexical, and SSE streaming — had to update its internals for React 18 concurrent mode. Zustand v4 changed its entire subscription mechanism. Lexical freezes its EditorState. If you understand *why* they made those changes, their APIs make sense. If you don't, they look like arbitrary design quirks.

## A concrete example

**The problem: state tearing**

Imagine a Zustand store backing a rich-text editor with a `documentTitle` field. React 18 starts rendering a component that reads `documentTitle`. Halfway through, React yields to a higher-priority update. While yielded, a mutation changes `documentTitle` in place. React resumes the original render and reads `documentTitle` again — but now gets a different value. The rendered output shows different title text in different parts of the component tree. This is tearing.

```typescript
// ❌ Mutable store — causes tearing in React 18
const buggyStore = {
  documentTitle: "Untitled",
  setTitle(title: string) {
    this.documentTitle = title // mutates in-place
  }
}

// ✅ Immutable store — safe under concurrent rendering
const useDocumentStore = create<{ title: string; setTitle: (t: string) => void }>()
  ((set) => ({
    title: "Untitled",
    setTitle: (title) => set({ title }), // produces a new state object
  }))
```

**`useSyncExternalStore`: React 18's contract with external state**

Zustand v4 replaced its custom subscription mechanism with `useSyncExternalStore`:

```typescript
// What Zustand does internally (simplified)
import { useSyncExternalStore } from "react"

function useStore<T>(store: Store<T>, selector: (state: T) => unknown) {
  return useSyncExternalStore(
    store.subscribe,    // called when state changes
    store.getSnapshot,  // must return the same reference if state is unchanged
    store.getSnapshot,  // server snapshot (same for SSR)
  )
}
```

`getSnapshot` must return a stable reference — the same object reference — if the state hasn't changed. This is the immutability contract: React can call `getSnapshot` multiple times during a concurrent render and trust that equal references mean equal values. [S002](https://react.dev/blog/2022/03/29/react-v18), [S009](https://github.com/pmndrs/zustand/blob/main/README.md)

**StrictMode double-invocation**

React 18's StrictMode invokes state initializers and some effects twice in development. This is not a bug — it's a deliberate mechanism to surface code that relies on mutation. If your state initializer has a side effect (mutates something), it will fail in development and work in production, revealing the bug before it ships. [S002](https://react.dev/blog/2022/03/29/react-v18)

## Key points

- React 18's concurrent renderer requires immutable state because it can hold multiple state snapshots simultaneously during interrupted renders
- `useSyncExternalStore` is the hook that formalises this contract — Zustand, Lexical, and AI streaming integration all use it
- StrictMode double-invocation in development is a feature that detects mutation-dependent code before it reaches production

## Go deeper

- [S002](https://react.dev/blog/2022/03/29/react-v18) — React 18 release notes: concurrent rendering, startTransition, useSyncExternalStore
- [S009](https://github.com/pmndrs/zustand/blob/main/README.md) — Zustand: why v4 switched to useSyncExternalStore

---

*[← Previous: Strict Mode Through Libraries](./L02-strict-mode-through-libraries.md)* · *[Next: Immutable Operations in Yjs →](./L04-immutable-ops-yjs-crdts.md)*

# PostgreSQL RLS + pgvector: One Table Definition, Three Jobs

**Module**: M04 · Single Source of Truth: Schema-as-Type Across the Stack
**Type**: applied
**Estimated time**: 15 minutes
**Claim**: C9, C10 — PostgreSQL RLS and pgvector each add a layer of enforcement to the same table definition

---

## The core idea

A single Drizzle table definition can simultaneously define: a relational schema with foreign key constraints, Row-Level Security policies that enforce tenant isolation automatically at the database layer, and a pgvector column with an HNSW index for ANN (approximate nearest neighbour) search. These aren't separate concerns bolted together — they're all properties of one table, one source of truth, and one TypeScript type.

## Why it matters

Meridian requires multi-tenant isolation: every query against workspace data must be scoped to the requesting tenant. If that scoping is only enforced at the application layer (Express middleware), a bug in one route could leak data across tenants. RLS enforces it at the database layer — even a misconfigured or missing middleware call can't bypass it. Understanding how Drizzle, the tenant middleware, and `SET LOCAL` connect is the difference between "tenant isolation we hope is correct" and "tenant isolation enforced by the database engine."

## A concrete example

**Step 1: Define the table with an embedding column**

```typescript
// server/src/db/schema.ts
import { pgTable, uuid, text, timestamp, index } from "drizzle-orm/pg-core"
import { vector } from "drizzle-orm/pg-core" // pgvector extension

export const memories = pgTable("memories", {
  id: uuid("id").defaultRandom().primaryKey(),
  userId: uuid("user_id").notNull().references(() => users.id),
  workspaceId: uuid("workspace_id").notNull().references(() => workspaces.id),
  content: text("content").notNull(),
  embedding: vector("embedding", { dimensions: 1536 }), // pgvector column
  createdAt: timestamp("created_at").defaultNow().notNull(),
}, (table) => ({
  // HNSW index for fast ANN search
  embeddingIdx: index("memories_embedding_idx")
    .using("hnsw", table.embedding.op("vector_cosine_ops")),
}))
```

**Step 2: Add RLS in a migration**

```sql
-- db/migrations/0005_add_rls_memories.sql
ALTER TABLE memories ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

-- Policy: queries only see rows where workspace_id matches the session variable
CREATE POLICY workspace_isolation ON memories
  USING (workspace_id = current_setting('app.current_workspace_id')::uuid);

-- The policy uses current_setting() — a PostgreSQL session variable
-- This variable is set per-connection by the tenant middleware
```

**Step 3: Set the session variable in every request**

```typescript
// server/src/middleware/tenant.ts
import { Request, Response, NextFunction } from "express"
import { db } from "../db"
import { sql } from "drizzle-orm"

export async function tenantMiddleware(
  req: Request,
  res: Response,
  next: NextFunction
): Promise<void> {
  // workspaceId comes from the verified JWT, never from req.body
  const workspaceId = req.jwtPayload?.workspaceId
  if (!workspaceId) {
    res.status(401).json({ error: "No workspace context" })
    return
  }

  // SET LOCAL scopes this to the current transaction only
  // If the transaction rolls back, the setting disappears
  await db.execute(sql`SET LOCAL app.current_workspace_id = ${workspaceId}`)
  req.workspace = { id: workspaceId }
  next()
}
```

**Step 4: Use the vector search — RLS applies automatically**

```typescript
// server/src/services/memory.ts
import { db } from "../db"
import { memories } from "../db/schema"
import { sql, and, eq } from "drizzle-orm"

export async function searchMemories(
  userId: string,
  workspaceId: string, // still include both filters — belt-and-suspenders
  queryEmbedding: number[],
  limit = 10,
) {
  // RLS policy enforces workspace_id filter automatically
  // The explicit filter here is belt-and-suspenders, required by Meridian rules
  const results = await db
    .select()
    .from(memories)
    .where(
      and(
        eq(memories.userId, userId),
        eq(memories.workspaceId, workspaceId),
      )
    )
    .orderBy(sql`embedding <=> ${JSON.stringify(queryEmbedding)}::vector`)
    .limit(limit)

  return results
}
```

**Why RLS + explicit WHERE is belt-and-suspenders**

RLS enforces the `workspace_id` filter at the database engine level — even if the application code forgets the `WHERE` clause. The explicit `workspaceId` filter in the application code means a bug in `SET LOCAL` (e.g., the middleware didn't run) would still fail safely, because the application-layer filter would return 0 rows rather than the wrong tenant's rows. Two independent mechanisms, each covering the other's failure mode. [S003](https://lexical.dev/docs/intro), [S004](https://docs.yjs.dev/)

## Key points

- RLS adds tenant isolation at the database engine layer; `SET LOCAL` is the mechanism that scopes it to the current transaction — if `SET LOCAL` didn't run, the query fails (RLS rejects `current_setting()` returning null)
- pgvector's HNSW index enables approximate nearest-neighbour search in `O(log n)` time; the exact SQL `<=>` operator is the cosine distance operator
- The Meridian rule requires *both* the RLS policy and the explicit `WHERE workspaceId =` filter — belt-and-suspenders because each covers the other's failure mode

## Go deeper

- [S003](https://lexical.dev/docs/intro) — PostgreSQL RLS: policy syntax, `current_setting()`, and `SET LOCAL` transaction scoping
- [S004](https://docs.yjs.dev/) — pgvector: HNSW vs IVFFlat, distance operators, and Drizzle integration

---

*[← Previous: Schema-as-Type](./L06-schema-as-type-drizzle-zod.md)* · *[Next: useSyncExternalStore →](./L08-usesyncexternalstore.md)*

# Vite SPA vs Next.js: Why SSR-First Frameworks Break Meridian

**Module**: M05 · React 18 + Zustand: The Client State Integration Contract
**Type**: debate
**Estimated time**: 15 minutes
**Claim**: C3 — Vite SPA is the architecturally correct choice for Meridian; Next.js App Router is not

---

## The core idea

Next.js App Router is designed around server-first rendering: components run on the server by default, data fetches happen at the server, and the client progressively hydrates. This architecture is optimal for content-heavy, SEO-important sites. Meridian is the opposite: a collaborative, real-time editor where the most complex components — Lexical's DOM, Yjs's WebSocket provider — cannot run on the server at all. Using Next.js for Meridian would not just be suboptimal; it would require working against the framework's design at every layer.

## Why it matters

The "just use Next.js" reflex is strong because Next.js has excellent DX and broad adoption. Understanding specifically *why* it breaks for Meridian's architecture prevents a costly decision made on familiarity. It also clarifies Vite's role: not a framework, but a build tool — giving you full control over what runs where.

## A concrete example

**Why Lexical cannot run on the server**

Lexical is a DOM-dependent editor. Its core architecture builds and manipulates a shadow node tree that maps to DOM nodes. It uses `document.createElement`, `MutationObserver`, and `Selection` APIs. None of these exist in Node.js.

```typescript
// Lexical source — creates DOM nodes during reconciliation
// This code path executes even during "passive" operations like read()
import { createEditor } from "lexical"

// ❌ In a Next.js Server Component (runs on Node.js):
// throws: "document is not defined"
const editor = createEditor({ namespace: "meridian" })

// ✅ In a client component (runs in the browser):
"use client"
const editor = createEditor({ namespace: "meridian" })
```

The Next.js workaround is `dynamic(() => import(...), { ssr: false })` — essentially opting out of SSR for the editor component. But if the editor is the core of your product and SSR is disabled for it, you've gained nothing from Next.js's primary feature.

**Why Y.WebsocketProvider cannot run on the server**

Yjs's `WebsocketProvider` connects to a WebSocket URL on instantiation. WebSocket connections are bi-directional, long-lived, and browser-native. Server Components don't persist between requests — they render once, return HTML or RSC payload, and exit. There's no socket lifecycle to manage.

```typescript
// server/src/sync-server.ts — Meridian runs its own ws server
import { WebSocketServer } from "ws"
import { setupWSConnection } from "y-websocket/bin/utils"

const wss = new WebSocketServer({ port: 1234 })

wss.on("connection", (ws, req) => {
  setupWSConnection(ws, req, { docName: req.url?.slice(1) })
})

// client/src/editor/CollaborativeEditor.tsx
"use client" // Next.js — must mark client
import { WebsocketProvider } from "y-websocket"
import { useEffect, useRef } from "react"

// Provider must be created inside useEffect — client-only, after mount
useEffect(() => {
  const provider = new WebsocketProvider(
    "ws://localhost:1234",
    documentId,
    ydoc,
  )
  return () => provider.destroy()
}, [documentId])
```

With Vite SPA, there's no server-side concept to worry about. Every component is a client component. The `useEffect` is still needed for cleanup, but there's no architectural overhead.

**What Vite SPA gives Meridian**

```
Vite SPA                           Next.js App Router
─────────────────────────────────  ──────────────────────────────────
All components run in browser      Components run on server by default
No SSR hydration complexity        Hydration mismatch errors possible
Full WebSocket lifecycle control   WebSocket requires client boundary
Lexical runs without workaround    Requires ssr: false for editor
y-websocket works as designed      Requires useEffect + "use client"
Build output: static files + API   Build output: server + static files
Deployment: CDN + Express          Deployment: Node.js server required
```

**The SPA trade-off Meridian accepts**

Vite SPA means no server-side rendering, so the initial page load is a blank HTML shell with a script tag. Search engines can't crawl Meridian's document content. For a workspace tool — authenticated, behind a login wall — this is correct: the content is private, SEO is irrelevant, and the faster initial interactive time from a CDN-served SPA outweighs the cost of no SSR. [S008](https://vite.dev/guide/why), [S007](https://github.com/pgvector/pgvector)

## Key points

- Lexical depends on `document`, `MutationObserver`, and `Selection` — APIs that don't exist in Node.js, making it fundamentally incompatible with Next.js Server Components
- Y.WebsocketProvider requires persistent connection lifecycle management that is incompatible with the stateless request/response model of Server Components
- Vite SPA is not a compromise — for Meridian (authenticated, real-time, collaborative), it's the architecturally correct choice: no SSR overhead, full client control, CDN-optimal static output

## Go deeper

- [S008](https://vite.dev/guide/why) — Vite: build pipeline, SPA mode, and HMR architecture
- [S007](https://github.com/pgvector/pgvector) — Lexical: DOM dependency and why SSR is unsupported

---

*[← Previous: useSyncExternalStore](./L08-usesyncexternalstore.md)* · *[Next: CRDTs vs OT →](./L10-crdts-vs-ot.md)*

# CRDTs vs OT: Why the Algorithm Choice Determines the Editor Choice

**Module**: M06 · Collaborative Editing: Yjs, CRDTs, and Lexical
**Type**: core
**Estimated time**: 15 minutes
**Claim**: C6 — The CRDT algorithm choice determines the editor choice; they cannot be separated

---

## The core idea

Operational Transformation (OT) and Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) are two algorithms for resolving concurrent edits in collaborative documents. They are not interchangeable choices for the same problem — they make different architectural commitments. OT requires a central server to order all operations. CRDTs do not. This difference is what connects the algorithm choice directly to the editor choice: Yjs (CRDT) has a first-class `@lexical/yjs` binding; Google Docs-style OT requires a central server that Meridian's architecture doesn't want.

## Why it matters

Knowing the difference between OT and CRDTs explains why `y-websocket` is a synchronisation *relay*, not a conflict resolution server. It explains why Meridian can support offline editing (edit while disconnected, sync when reconnected). And it explains why the binding quality matters: `@lexical/yjs` is maintained by the Lexical team, not a community member.

## A concrete example

**Operational Transformation — the central authority model**

OT transforms concurrent operations so they can be applied in any order while producing the same result. The key constraint: with two concurrent operations `a` and `b`, you need to compute `a'` (what `a` becomes after `b` has been applied) — the transformation function. For complex document structures, this function is notoriously difficult to implement correctly.

The deeper problem: OT transformation functions require global knowledge of all pending operations to compute correctly. This requires a central server that sees all operations and performs the transformations.

```
Client A:  insert("e") at index 2      →  sends to server
Client B:  delete(2, 1) at index 2     →  sends to server

Server:    OT transforms both operations against each other
           and sends transformed versions to each client
           
Without server: Client A and B might arrive at different states
               because each computed the wrong transformation
```

**CRDTs — the convergence-without-coordination model**

CRDTs provide a mathematical property: for any two sets of operations applied in any order, the result is always the same. This works because each operation carries enough information (its own identity: a Lamport clock + peer ID) to be correctly positioned relative to other operations without coordination.

```
Client A:  {id: [3, "A"], left: "o", right: "!", content: "e"}
           // Insert 'e' after the 'o' in "Hello!" 
           
Client B:  {id: [3, "B"], left: "o", right: "!", content: "W"}
           // Insert 'W' after the 'o' in "Hello!"

Both clients receive both operations:
A applies B after its own: "Heleo!" → "HeleoW!" (positions from identities)
B applies A after its own: "HelloW!" → "HelloeW!" 

Hmm, different order... but because IDs are unique and both clients
use same deterministic conflict resolution (e.g., sort by peer ID),
both arrive at: "HelloeW!" or "HelloWe!" consistently
```

The point: no server needed to decide the order. Both peers converge independently.

**Why this makes y-websocket a relay, not a resolver**

```yaml
# y-websocket server — from Meridian's docker-compose.yml
yjs:
  image: yjs/y-websocket
  ports:
    - "1234:1234"
  # Note: no complex OT transformation logic here
  # y-websocket simply relays Y.Doc updates between peers
  # Conflict resolution happens inside y.js (CRDT) on each client
```

The y-websocket server is a message relay — it forwards Yjs update binaries from one peer to all others. If peer A and peer B are both offline and make concurrent edits, they each produce Yjs updates. When both reconnect, y-websocket relays their updates to each other. Each peer applies both sets of updates using Yjs's CRDT algorithm and converges to the same state — without the server having seen the conflict.

**The chain: offline requirement → Yjs → Lexical**

```
Requirement: support offline editing (edit while disconnected)
  ↓
Requires: algorithm that doesn't need a central authority
  ↓
Algorithm: CRDT (specifically Yjs's YATA algorithm)
  ↓
Library: Yjs (reference CRDT implementation for collaborative text)
  ↓
Editor requirement: first-class Yjs integration, not a community binding
  ↓
Editor: Lexical (official @lexical/yjs package, maintained by Meta)
  ↓
Not: ProseMirror (y-prosemirror is third-party, not Yjs-first)
  ↓
Not: TipTap (uses y-prosemirror under the hood for Yjs)
```

[S006](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/ddl-rowsecurity.html), [S007](https://github.com/pgvector/pgvector)

## Key points

- OT requires a central server that serializes and transforms all operations; CRDT convergence is guaranteed client-side, making CRDTs the only practical choice for offline-capable collaborative editing
- `y-websocket` is a relay server, not a conflict resolver — CRDT resolution happens inside Yjs on each client; the server just ensures all clients eventually receive all updates
- The chain is deterministic: offline requirement → CRDT → Yjs → Lexical (because `@lexical/yjs` is first-class); choosing ProseMirror or TipTap would require a third-party binding that the Yjs team doesn't maintain

## Go deeper

- [S006](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/ddl-rowsecurity.html) — Yjs: YATA algorithm, convergence proofs, and provider architecture
- [S007](https://github.com/pgvector/pgvector) — Lexical: `@lexical/yjs` integration and the offline sync lifecycle

# pnpm + Docker Compose + Turborepo: Three Scopes, Not Three Options

**Module**: M09 · Monorepo Tooling: Three Scopes, One Project
**Type**: applied
**Estimated time**: 15 minutes
**Claim**: C15, C16 — pnpm workspaces, Turborepo, and Docker Compose operate at distinct scopes and are complementary, not competing

---

## The core idea

Three tools appear in Meridian's monorepo that developers often conflate: pnpm workspaces manages the *package registry* (which packages exist, how they link to each other, what their dependencies are), Turborepo manages the *task graph* (which build/test/lint tasks depend on each other, what can be cached), and Docker Compose manages the *service runtime* (which processes run in which containers, with which health checks and network rules). They operate at entirely different scopes. You don't choose between them — you add each one when its specific problem appears.

## Why it matters

The common confusion: "we already have pnpm workspaces, why do we need Turborepo?" or "why Docker Compose if we have a monorepo?" Understanding the scope boundaries prevents adding the wrong tool for a problem and helps you identify the right signal that triggers adding the next tool.

## A concrete example

**Scope 1: pnpm workspaces — package registry**

```yaml
# pnpm-workspace.yaml — at repo root
packages:
  - "client"
  - "server"
  - "db"
```

```json
// server/package.json — workspace:* linking
{
  "name": "@meridian/server",
  "dependencies": {
    "@meridian/db": "workspace:*"  // links to the local db/ package
  }
}
```

pnpm workspaces provides:
- A single `node_modules` at the root (hoisted dependencies)
- `workspace:*` links between packages — no publishing needed
- `--filter` to scope commands to specific packages

```bash
# Run only the server package's dev command
pnpm --filter @meridian/server dev

# Install a dependency in the client only
pnpm --filter @meridian/client add @lexical/react

# Type-check all packages simultaneously
pnpm --filter "./packages/**" typecheck
```

What pnpm workspaces does NOT do: it has no concept of task dependencies. Running `pnpm --filter @meridian/server build` does not know to first build `@meridian/db`. That's Turborepo's job.

**Scope 2: Turborepo — task graph and caching**

The observable signal that triggers adding Turborepo: you notice that `pnpm build` runs `db` and `server` and `client` builds sequentially when `db` could finish before `server` starts, saving minutes.

```json
// turbo.json — at repo root
{
  "$schema": "https://turbo.build/schema.json",
  "pipeline": {
    "build": {
      "dependsOn": ["^build"],  // Run build in dependencies first
      "outputs": ["dist/**", ".next/**"]
    },
    "typecheck": {
      "dependsOn": ["^build"]   // Types from db must be built before server typechecks
    },
    "dev": {
      "cache": false,           // Dev servers should not be cached
      "persistent": true        // Long-running processes
    },
    "test": {
      "dependsOn": ["build"],
      "outputs": ["coverage/**"]
    }
  }
}
```

```bash
# Turborepo runs builds in dependency order, in parallel where possible
# db builds first, then server + client in parallel
turbo build

# Turborepo caches: if source files haven't changed, returns cache hit
# Re-running after no changes: all packages restored from cache instantly
turbo build  # → "cache hit" for unchanged packages
```

**Scope 3: Docker Compose — service runtime**

```yaml
# infra/docker-compose.yml
version: "3.9"

services:
  postgres:
    image: ankane/pgvector:latest
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: meridian
      POSTGRES_USER: meridian
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ../db/schema.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/01-schema.sql
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U meridian"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 10
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"

  yjs:
    image: yjs/y-websocket
    ports:
      - "1234:1234"
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy

  server:
    build:
      context: ../server
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.dev
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://meridian:${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}@postgres:5432/meridian
      ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY}
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    volumes:
      - ../server:/app
      - /app/node_modules
    ports:
      - "3001:3001"

  client:
    build:
      context: ../client
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.dev
    ports:
      - "5173:5173"
    volumes:
      - ../client:/app
      - /app/node_modules

volumes:
  postgres_data:
```

Docker Compose provides:
- Service networking (containers communicate by service name: `postgres`, `server`, `yjs`)
- Health checks — `depends_on: condition: service_healthy` waits for postgres to be ready before starting server
- Volume mounts for hot reload (`/app/node_modules` exclusion prevents host modules from overwriting container modules)

**The three scopes mapped to one question each**

| Tool | Question it answers | When to add it |
|---|---|---|
| pnpm workspaces | "How are my packages linked and installed?" | Day one — before writing any code |
| Turborepo | "Which build tasks depend on each other, and what can be cached?" | When you have ≥2 packages with build steps that depend on each other |
| Docker Compose | "How do I start all services (DB, API, editor server, client) with a single command?" | Day one — when you need postgres + server + client to run together |

**The Makefile as the unified entry point**

```makefile
# infra/Makefile
.PHONY: dev build test migrate seed clean

dev:
	cd infra && docker compose up --build

build:
	turbo build

test:
	turbo test

migrate:
	pnpm --filter @meridian/server db:migrate

seed:
	pnpm --filter @meridian/server db:seed

clean:
	docker compose -f infra/docker-compose.yml down -v
	find . -name "dist" -type d -exec rm -rf {} +
```

Running `make dev` starts Docker Compose (all services with health checks). Running `make build` triggers Turborepo (builds all packages in dependency order). The tools never overlap. [S015](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8725), [S016](https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet.html)

## Key points

- pnpm workspaces = package registry (installation, linking); Turborepo = task graph (ordering, caching); Docker Compose = service runtime (health checks, networking) — three non-overlapping scopes
- The signal to add Turborepo: sequential package builds where dependency order matters and build time has become a bottleneck — not a day-one tool, but a natural addition as the monorepo grows
- Docker Compose health checks (`condition: service_healthy`) are required — `depends_on` without a health check condition doesn't wait for the service to be ready, only for the container to start

## Go deeper

- [S015](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8725) — pnpm workspaces: `workspace:*` linking, `--filter`, and hoisting behaviour
- [S016](https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet.html) — Turborepo: pipeline configuration, `dependsOn: ["^build"]`, and remote caching

cards.front: What is the difference between enabling `strict: true` vs enabling individual flags like `noImplicitAny: true` in tsconfig.json?
cards.back: `strict: true` activates nine flags simultaneously — it is a shorthand, not a separate feature. Enabling individual flags selectively gives weaker guarantees because some flags depend on others being active (e.g., `strictNullChecks` has limited value without `noImplicitAny`).

cards.front: Which two strict sub-flags are most critical for the Meridian stack to function correctly, and why?
cards.back: `noImplicitAny` — prevents `z.infer<>` from silently returning `any` instead of the derived type. `strictNullChecks` — makes `null` and `undefined` distinct types, so optional API response fields require explicit handling rather than silently passing type checks.

cards.front: Why does Vite require `isolatedModules: true` in tsconfig.json?
cards.back: Vite uses esbuild which transpiles each file independently, without cross-file type information. `isolatedModules: true` ensures every TypeScript file can be safely transpiled in isolation — banning `const enum` declarations and requiring `import type` for type-only imports.

cards.front: What happens to `z.infer<typeof schema>` when TypeScript strict mode is disabled?
cards.back: For certain schema shapes involving conditional types in Zod's internals, `z.infer<>` returns `any` instead of the derived type — with no compiler error. The type silently degrades, giving false confidence that every validated value is correctly typed.

cards.front: Why does Zustand use the curried `create<State>()(() => ...)` pattern instead of the simpler `create<State>(() => ...)`?
cards.back: TypeScript's generic inference requires strict mode to correctly resolve the `State` generic from both the explicit type parameter and the callback simultaneously. The extra `()` curries the call, forcing TypeScript to resolve the generic correctly. Without it, `set` types as `any`.

cards.front: How does Drizzle's `$inferSelect` differ from Prisma's approach to deriving TypeScript types from a table definition?
cards.back: Drizzle derives types structurally at compile time from the table definition object — no codegen step, no separate `.prisma` file. `typeof memories.$inferSelect` produces the full query result type directly. Prisma requires a codegen step after every schema change to materialize types into generated client code.

cards.front: What is state tearing in React 18, and what causes it?
cards.back: Tearing is when different parts of the UI simultaneously reflect different versions of the same state. It is caused by mutable state: React 18's concurrent renderer can interrupt a render mid-flight and resume later; if state mutates between the interruption and resumption, subsequent reads return a different value than earlier reads in the same render.

cards.front: Why did Zustand switch to `useSyncExternalStore` in v4?
cards.back: The previous implementation used `useState` + `useEffect` to sync external state into React. React 18's concurrent renderer can gap this pair — a render could start, yield, external state mutates, render resumes with stale data. `useSyncExternalStore` makes state reads atomic: React calls `getSnapshot()` during the render itself, not before it.

cards.front: What is the React 18 StrictMode double-invocation behaviour, and what bug class does it detect?
cards.back: In development, StrictMode invokes state initializers and some effects twice deliberately. This detects code that relies on mutation: if a state initializer mutates something, double-invocation will produce incorrect results in development and correct results in production, surfacing the mutation dependency before it ships.

cards.front: What does it mean for a CRDT operation to be commutative, and why does it matter for offline editing?
cards.back: Commutativity means: applying operation A then B produces the same result as applying B then A. For offline editing, this means two peers can each accumulate operations while disconnected and, when they reconnect, apply each other's operations in any order and still arrive at the same document state — no central server needed to serialize the order.

cards.front: What does Lexical's `EditorState` and Yjs's operations have in common architecturally?
cards.back: Both are immutable values. Lexical's `EditorState` is frozen after every `editor.update()` call — mutations outside this context throw at runtime. Yjs operations are immutable value objects carrying identity (clientId + Lamport clock) so they can be applied in any order. The `@lexical/yjs` binding bridges two immutable systems: EditorState diffs become Yjs operations.

cards.front: Why can't you mutate a `Y.Text` value directly with something like `ytext.content = 'Hello'`?
cards.back: Yjs has no mutable setter on shared types. All changes must go through operation methods (`ytext.insert()`, `ytext.delete()`) wrapped in `ydoc.transact()`. Each call produces an immutable operation object with a unique identity. Direct mutation would bypass the operation identity mechanism that makes CRDT convergence work.

cards.front: What single question resolves every major technology choice in Meridian?
cards.back: "What mechanism does this tool expose directly, and what does the alternative hide?" Control (direct access, full flexibility, more code) vs Abstraction (DX, hidden mechanism, locked into the abstraction's model). The correct choice depends on whether the hidden mechanism is one Meridian needs direct access to.

cards.front: Why does Drizzle beat Prisma for Meridian specifically — what are the three technical requirements that rule Prisma out?
cards.back: 1. `SET LOCAL app.current_workspace_id = ${id}` for RLS — idiomatic in Drizzle's `db.execute(sql`...`)`, awkward in Prisma's `$executeRaw`. 2. Native pgvector column types — Drizzle exposes them as raw type definitions; Prisma requires extension workarounds with type loss. 3. Zero codegen — Drizzle table definitions produce TypeScript types structurally; Prisma requires a codegen step after every schema change.

cards.front: Express benchmarks at ~15k req/s vs Fastify's ~65k req/s. Why is Express still the correct choice for Meridian?
cards.back: Meridian's per-request latency is dominated by PostgreSQL queries (5–50ms) and Anthropic API calls (100–5000ms). When the bottleneck is database or AI latency — not HTTP processing — a 4× framework throughput difference produces no measurable user experience improvement. Express wins on middleware composability and team familiarity.

cards.front: When would Prisma be the better choice over Drizzle?
cards.back: When the application doesn't need custom SQL extensions (no pgvector, no RLS via `SET LOCAL`), the team is less comfortable writing SQL, nested write handling and automatic join management matter more than explicit SQL control, and the ~40MB binary engine cost is acceptable. Prisma is excellent for standard CRUD SaaS without custom PostgreSQL features.

cards.front: What does `createInsertSchema(table)` from `drizzle-zod` produce, and why is it useful for API routes?
cards.back: It produces a Zod schema that mirrors the table's insert type — automatically excluding server-generated fields and matching column types. Used in an Express route, it means a single table definition change propagates through to the validation schema and the TypeScript type at the call site with zero manual updates.

cards.front: Why should `workspaceId` always be omitted from a Zod insert schema for workspace-scoped routes?
cards.back: To enforce tenant isolation: `workspaceId` must always come from the verified JWT payload (set by tenant middleware), never from user-supplied request body. `.omit({ workspaceId: true })` on the schema makes the field absent from validation, so users cannot supply it. The server always sets it from `req.workspace.id`.

cards.front: What does `SET LOCAL app.current_workspace_id = $1` do, and why must it run before any workspace-scoped query?
cards.back: `SET LOCAL` sets a PostgreSQL session variable scoped to the current transaction only — the value disappears when the transaction ends. PostgreSQL RLS policies use `current_setting('app.current_workspace_id')::uuid` to filter rows. If `SET LOCAL` hasn't run, the RLS policy rejects the query. Running it after a query would mean the policy had no context to use.

cards.front: Why does Meridian use both RLS policies AND explicit `WHERE workspaceId = ?` filters in queries, even though RLS alone would enforce isolation?
cards.back: Belt-and-suspenders defense-in-depth: if the tenant middleware had a bug and `SET LOCAL` didn't run, RLS would reject the query with an error (not a data leak). If RLS were somehow misconfigured, the explicit application-layer filter would still scope the result correctly. Each layer covers the other's failure mode.

cards.front: What is an HNSW index, and why does pgvector use it for embedding search?
cards.back: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is an approximate nearest-neighbour algorithm with `O(log n)` search time. pgvector uses it to find embeddings similar to a query vector via cosine distance (`<=>`) without scanning every row. 'Approximate' means it may miss a few true nearest neighbours for significant speed gains — acceptable for RAG retrieval.

cards.front: What are the two required arguments to `useSyncExternalStore`, and what does React use each one for?
cards.back: `subscribe(listener)` — React calls this to register a callback it will invoke when the store changes, triggering a re-render. `getSnapshot()` — React calls this during rendering to read the current state. If `getSnapshot` returns the same reference between calls, React skips reconciliation. Both must be stable: subscribe for cleanup, getSnapshot for reference equality.

cards.front: When does a Zustand selector cause unnecessary re-renders, and how does `useShallow` fix it?
cards.back: When a selector returns a new object reference on every call (e.g., `(state) => ({ a: state.a, b: state.b })`), React sees a new reference on every snapshot read and re-renders even when values are unchanged. `useShallow` wraps the selector in a shallow equality check — it only triggers a re-render when any selected property value actually changes.

cards.front: Describe the data flow when an SSE streaming AI response connects to a React component via Zustand in React 18.
cards.back: 1. `EventSource` fires `onmessage` with a delta chunk. 2. `appendChunk(delta)` calls Zustand `set()` producing a new state object (not mutation). 3. Zustand notifies its `useSyncExternalStore` subscriber. 4. React calls `getSnapshot()`, sees a new reference. 5. React re-renders the component with the updated accumulating content. No tearing possible because each step produces a new immutable state object.

cards.front: Why can't Lexical be used in a Next.js Server Component?
cards.back: Lexical's core relies on `document.createElement`, `MutationObserver`, and `Selection` — DOM APIs that don't exist in Node.js. Even passive operations like `editor.read()` trigger Lexical's DOM-dependent reconciler. Next.js Server Components run on Node.js. Lexical must be in a Client Component with `ssr: false` or in a Vite SPA where every component runs in the browser.

cards.front: Under what conditions would Next.js App Router be the better choice over Vite SPA for a workspace product like Meridian?
cards.back: If the workspace had public-facing pages that needed SEO (landing page, public shared documents, blog). Next.js's SSR/SSG would serve crawlable HTML for those routes. For Meridian's authenticated workspace — private content, no SEO value, real-time real features — these benefits don't apply and the SSR overhead is pure cost.

cards.front: Why can a `WebsocketProvider` from `y-websocket` not be instantiated in a Next.js Server Component lifecycle?
cards.back: Y.WebsocketProvider creates a persistent WebSocket connection on instantiation and manages a connection lifecycle (reconnect, sync, awareness updates). Server Components are stateless — they render once and exit. There is no lifecycle to maintain a persistent connection in. WebSocket connections require client-side lifecycle management inside `useEffect`.

cards.front: What is the fundamental difference between OT (Operational Transformation) and CRDTs for collaborative editing?
cards.back: OT resolves concurrent conflicts by computing transformed operations — but this requires a central server that sees all operations in order to compute transformations correctly. CRDTs resolve conflicts using mathematical properties (commutativity/idempotency) embedded in each operation's identity — no central authority needed. Two CRDT peers can converge independently with just the operations, in any order.

cards.front: What is `y-websocket`'s role in Meridian's collaboration architecture — specifically, what does it NOT do?
cards.back: `y-websocket` is a relay server — it forwards binary Yjs update messages between peers in the same room. It does NOT perform conflict resolution, does NOT determine operation order for correctness, and does NOT act as source of truth. CRDT convergence happens inside each client's `Y.Doc`. The server just ensures all clients eventually receive all updates.

cards.front: Trace the chain of decisions from 'Meridian requires offline editing' to 'the editor must be Lexical'.
cards.back: 1. Offline editing → algorithm that doesn't need central authority for correctness. 2. → CRDT (specifically Yjs's YATA algorithm). 3. → Yjs library. 4. → Editor needs first-class Yjs integration (not a community binding). 5. → Lexical (`@lexical/yjs` maintained by Meta). Not ProseMirror (`y-prosemirror` is third-party, not Lexical-team-maintained).

cards.front: What is Yjs `awareness`, and how does it differ from the document content CRDT?
cards.back: Awareness is ephemeral peer state — cursor positions, user name/colour, online status. It synchronises between peers via the provider but is NOT stored in the CRDT document. When a peer disconnects, their awareness state is cleaned up automatically. Document content is CRDT-persisted; awareness is transient and presence-only.

cards.front: Why must `CollaborationPlugin` in Lexical receive `editorState: null` in the initial `LexicalComposer` config?
cards.back: When `CollaborationPlugin` is active, the editor's initial state comes from the Yjs document — either the existing document state from the sync server or a bootstrapped empty state. Setting `editorState` to anything other than `null` would initialize Lexical's own state, which would then conflict with the state coming from Yjs.

cards.front: What happens to changes made in Lexical + Yjs when the user is offline, and how do they sync when connectivity returns?
cards.back: Yjs continues accepting local operations offline — they're stored in the in-memory `Y.Doc`. When the WebSocket reconnects, `WebsocketProvider` sends all pending Yjs update binaries to the relay server, which distributes them to other peers. Each peer's `Y.Doc` applies the updates using CRDT merge and converges to the same document state. No manual handling required.

cards.front: Name the four stages of a RAG pipeline and what happens at each stage.
cards.back: 1. **Ingestion**: chunk text → embed each chunk → store (content + embedding vector) in pgvector. 2. **Retrieval**: embed the query → find nearest chunks via `ORDER BY embedding <=> queryVector` LIMIT N. 3. **Augmentation**: inject retrieved chunks into the system prompt. 4. **Generation**: send augmented prompt to the LLM and stream the response.

cards.front: Where should retrieved RAG context be injected — in the system prompt or in the user message — and why?
cards.back: In the **system prompt**. Injecting into the user message would pollute the conversation history and cause the context to repeat in every subsequent turn. The system prompt persists across all turns, making the retrieved context available to every response in the conversation without repeating it. It also keeps the user message clean for display in the UI.

cards.front: The Meridian vector search pattern requires both `userId` and `workspaceId` as filters. Why is scoping by `workspaceId` alone insufficient?
cards.back: A workspace can have multiple users. Filtering only by `workspaceId` would allow User A to retrieve memories ingested by User B in the same workspace. Both filters are required: `workspaceId` scopes to the tenant, `userId` scopes to the individual's personal memory within that tenant. This matches the security model: personal AI memory is private even within a shared workspace.

cards.front: What does the Vercel AI SDK provide that the direct Anthropic SDK does not, and when does that value proposition apply?
cards.back: The Vercel AI SDK provides provider portability — a single API surface that works with OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and others, making it easy to switch providers. This matters when an application needs multi-provider support. For Meridian (single provider: Anthropic), there is no provider to switch to, so the portability adds indirection without benefit.

cards.front: What are the three core types you need to understand from the Anthropic TypeScript SDK to write any AI route in Meridian?
cards.back: `MessageCreateParams` — the request shape (model, max_tokens, system, messages array). `Message` — the non-streaming response (content array, usage, stop_reason). `MessageStreamEvent` — the streaming event discriminated union used with `client.messages.stream()`, where `content_block_delta` events carry text chunks.

cards.front: How does tool calling work in the Anthropic SDK — what does the model return, and what do you do with it?
cards.back: The model responds with `stop_reason: 'tool_use'` and a `ToolUseBlock` in `response.content` containing the tool name and parsed `input` object. Your code executes the tool and sends the result back by appending a new message with `role: 'user'` and `content: [{ type: 'tool_result', tool_use_id: block.id, content: result }]`, then calling `messages.create()` again.

cards.front: What is the correct order of Meridian's four security middleware layers, and why can't steps 2 and 3 be reversed?
cards.back: Order: Zod body validation → JWT verification (extracts `workspaceId`) → `SET LOCAL` (sets RLS context) → route handler. Steps 2 and 3 cannot be reversed because `SET LOCAL` requires `workspaceId` from the verified JWT. If `SET LOCAL` ran first, it would have no workspace context to set, and RLS would reject every query.

cards.front: Why must `workspaceId` in tenant middleware come from `req.jwtPayload`, not `req.body` or `req.query`?
cards.back: User-supplied values in `req.body` or `req.query` cannot be trusted — a malicious user could supply any `workspaceId` and access another tenant's data. `req.jwtPayload.workspaceId` was verified by the JWT signature and expiry — it was issued by the server for that specific user's authorised workspace. This is the core tenant isolation guarantee.

cards.front: What does Zod's `safeParse` return when validation fails, and how should an Express route respond?
cards.back: `safeParse` returns `{ success: false, error: ZodError }`. The route should call `parsed.error.flatten()` to get a structured `{ fieldErrors: {...}, formErrors: [...] }` object and return `res.status(400).json({ errors: parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors })`. Never call `next()` on validation failure — terminate the request immediately.

cards.front: What does bcrypt's `cost` (rounds) parameter control, and what is the practical effect of increasing it by 1?
cards.back: `cost` is a power-of-two exponent for the number of bcrypt iterations: cost 12 = 4,096 iterations, cost 13 = 8,192 iterations. Each increment doubles the hashing time. At cost 12 on a 2024 server: ~250–400ms per hash. Increasing to 13 doubles that to ~500–800ms. The tradeoff is slower logins in exchange for making offline brute-force attacks proportionally harder.

cards.front: What makes Argon2id preferable to bcrypt for new systems, and why does Meridian still use bcrypt?
cards.back: Argon2id is memory-hard (configurable RAM usage, ~19MB recommended) making GPU-parallel cracking much more expensive than bcrypt, which fits in SRAM and is more GPU-friendly. Meridian uses bcrypt because: it's within OWASP's acceptable range at cost 12, has extensive Node.js production history, and requires no native bindings. Argon2id is the upgrade path if the threat model warrants it.

cards.front: Describe the `alg:none` JWT attack and the one-line fix that prevents it.
cards.back: An attacker crafts a JWT with `"alg": "none"` in the header and no signature. If `jwt.verify()` is called without specifying allowed algorithms, some implementations accept `alg:none` and skip signature verification entirely — accepting the forged token. Fix: `jwt.verify(token, secret, { algorithms: ['HS256'] })` — explicitly allowlisting the expected algorithm.

cards.front: Describe the RS256 → HS256 algorithm confusion attack and how `algorithms: ['HS256']` prevents it.
cards.back: If a server normally uses RS256 and publishes its public key, an attacker can create an HS256 token using that public key as the HMAC secret. If `jwt.verify()` accepts both RS256 and HS256, it might verify the attacker's HMAC signature using the public key and accept it. Specifying `algorithms: ['HS256']` with a secret-only path prevents this: only HS256 tokens can be verified, with only the secret.

cards.front: What are the three distinct scopes of pnpm workspaces, Turborepo, and Docker Compose — one sentence each?
cards.back: **pnpm workspaces**: manages the package registry — which packages exist, how they link, and what their dependencies are. **Turborepo**: manages the task graph — which build/test/lint tasks depend on each other and what can be cached. **Docker Compose**: manages the service runtime — which processes run in which containers, with what health checks and network topology.

cards.front: What does `workspace:*` mean in a pnpm package.json dependency, and what problem does it solve?
cards.back: `workspace:*` tells pnpm to link to the local workspace package at the current version, instead of fetching from a registry. It solves cross-package references in a monorepo: `@meridian/server` can depend on `@meridian/db` as if it were a published package, but changes to `db/` are immediately available to `server/` without publishing or version bumping.

cards.front: What is the observable signal that should trigger adding Turborepo to a pnpm workspace?
cards.back: When build tasks across packages have dependency relationships AND the sequential execution time is noticeably impacting developer feedback loops. Specifically: when `pnpm build` runs `@meridian/db` first (correct) but then runs `@meridian/server` and `@meridian/client` sequentially even though they could build in parallel after `db` finishes. Turborepo's `dependsOn: ['^build']` pipeline enables this parallelism with caching.

cards.front: Why does Docker Compose's `depends_on` need a `condition: service_healthy` qualifier, and what happens without it?
cards.back: Without `condition: service_healthy`, `depends_on` only waits for the dependent container to *start* (the process to fork) — not for it to be *ready* (accepting connections). For `postgres`, the container starts in milliseconds but PostgreSQL may take several seconds to initialize. Without the health check condition, `server` may attempt to connect to postgres before it's ready, failing with a connection error.

cards.front: When would you choose Turborepo's Remote Cache feature, and what problem does it solve that local caching doesn't?
cards.back: Remote Cache shares build artifacts across machines — different developers' local machines and CI servers all share the same cache. Without Remote Cache, each machine builds from scratch even if another machine already built the same commit. Remote Cache is warranted when CI builds are slow and the team has multiple developers frequently hitting cold cache builds for the same code.

cards.front: Why is pgvector's suitability for Meridian a moderate-confidence claim rather than strong — what is the unresolved question?
cards.back: No source provides a quantified performance threshold: at what number of embeddings per workspace does pgvector's HNSW index degrade to unacceptable query latency compared to Pinecone or Weaviate? The claim that pgvector is 'correct for current scale' is qualified — if Meridian grew to millions of embeddings per workspace, this would need benchmarking. Gap G001 captures this unresolved question.

cards.front: OWASP ranks Argon2id above bcrypt as the preferred algorithm for new systems. So why does Meridian mandate bcrypt? Is this a security weakness?
cards.back: bcrypt cost 12 is within OWASP's acceptable range — classified as 'legacy' (not insecure), just not optimal. Meridian chose bcrypt for library maturity and simplicity (no native bindings). The risk delta between bcrypt cost 12 and Argon2id is small at Meridian's threat model. The upgrade path exists: Argon2id via the `argon2` npm package, re-hashing on next login. Not a weakness, but a documented tradeoff.

cards.front: The direct Anthropic SDK is correct for Meridian today. When would this claim reverse, and what would the migration require?
cards.back: The claim reverses if Meridian adds a second LLM provider. The direct SDK is not provider-agnostic — every call site uses Anthropic-specific types and streaming protocol. Migrating to Vercel AI SDK would require rewriting all AI routes (different type signatures, different streaming event shapes, different tool calling interface). It's a significant refactor, not a config change. This architectural risk should be surfaced when evaluating the single-provider commitment.

cards.front: What is `useUnknownInCatchVariables`, and why does it matter for error handling in Meridian's server routes?
cards.back: One of strict mode's nine flags: before this flag, caught exceptions had type `any`. With it, `catch (e)` gives `e` the type `unknown`. This forces explicit type narrowing before using the error: `if (e instanceof Error) { ... }`. In Express error handlers and async route wrappers, this prevents accidentally treating unknown error shapes as typed Error objects.

cards.front: Show the three-step Drizzle + drizzle-zod pattern for a type-safe Express route that creates a document.
cards.back: 1. **Table definition**: `pgTable('documents', { id: uuid().primaryKey(), workspaceId: uuid().notNull(), title: text().notNull() })`. 2. **Schema derivation**: `const CreateSchema = createInsertSchema(documents).omit({ id: true, workspaceId: true })`. 3. **Route usage**: `const parsed = CreateSchema.safeParse(req.body); if (!parsed.success) return res.status(400)...; db.insert(documents).values({ ...parsed.data, workspaceId: req.workspace.id })`.

cards.front: Why must a Zustand store's `set()` produce a *new state object* rather than mutating the existing one?
cards.back: Because Zustand uses `useSyncExternalStore`, and `getSnapshot()` must return the same reference if state hasn't changed. Mutation would mean `getSnapshot()` returns the same reference even after change — React would see no new reference and skip re-rendering. Producing a new object ensures reference inequality → `getSnapshot` returns new reference → React re-renders.

cards.front: CRDTs eliminate the need for a central server for *correctness*. What do CRDTs still need a central server for in practice?
cards.back: **Peer discovery** — how does Peer A know Peer B's address? **Persistence** — who stores the document when all peers are offline? **Catch-up efficiency** — when a peer reconnects after weeks, receiving every individual operation is slow; a persisted snapshot is faster. These are practical concerns (infrastructure), not algorithmic. Meridian's `y-websocket` server handles relay and can be extended for persistence without affecting CRDT correctness.

cards.front: What is the `getSnapshot` immutability contract in `useSyncExternalStore`, and what breaks if it's violated?
cards.back: `getSnapshot` must return the **same object reference** if state hasn't changed. React calls it multiple times during concurrent rendering and uses reference equality to determine if a re-render is needed. If `getSnapshot` returns a new reference on every call (mutation pattern), React will perpetually think state changed and re-render infinitely. If it returns old references after mutation, React won't re-render when it should.

cards.front: What is the pgvector SQL operator for cosine similarity search, and what does the complete retrieval query look like?
cards.back: The operator is `<=>` (cosine distance — lower is more similar). Complete query: `SELECT content FROM memories WHERE user_id = $1 AND workspace_id = $2 ORDER BY embedding <=> $3::vector LIMIT 10`. The `$3` value is the query embedding as a JSON array cast to `::vector`. RLS policy on `workspace_id` also applies automatically.

cards.front: What is the minimum JWT secret length for HS256, and what attack does a short secret enable?
cards.back: Minimum 32 characters (256 bits). A shorter secret can be brute-forced: an attacker who obtains a valid JWT can try all possible secrets of the same length offline at millions of attempts per second. A 32-character random secret has 256 bits of entropy — brute-force is computationally infeasible. Use `openssl rand -base64 32` to generate a compliant secret.

cards.front: What is a CRDT tombstone, and what is the practical cost it imposes on long-lived Yjs documents?
cards.back: A tombstone is the record of a deleted item in a CRDT. CRDTs cannot truly delete an operation — the operation identity must remain to allow correct convergence with peers who haven't received the delete yet. Tombstones accumulate over time and increase the in-memory size of a Y.Doc. Very long-lived documents with many edits and deletions can become slow to load without a periodic snapshot-and-GC protocol.

cards.front: When would LangChain be the right choice over the direct Anthropic SDK?
cards.back: When you need complex agent orchestration that the direct SDK can't express cleanly: multi-step ReAct loops, automatic tool retry logic, chained prompts with conditional routing (LCEL), or a multi-provider abstraction where the LLM can be swapped. LangChain's overhead is justified when these patterns are core requirements. For Meridian (single provider, custom RAG pipeline already built), none of these conditions hold.

cards.front: Name the correct Drizzle syntax for defining a pgvector column with 1536 dimensions in a table definition.
cards.back: `embedding: vector('embedding', { dimensions: 1536 })` — imported from `drizzle-orm/pg-core`. The HNSW index for this column is defined in the table's third argument callback: `embeddingIdx: index('memories_embedding_idx').using('hnsw', table.embedding.op('vector_cosine_ops'))`.

cards.front: What purpose does `provider.awareness.setLocalStateField('user', { name, color })` serve, and when should it be called?
cards.back: It publishes this peer's presence metadata — name and cursor colour — to all connected peers in the same Yjs room. Other peers receive this via `provider.awareness.on('change', ...)` and can render a cursor indicator for each remote peer. It should be called immediately after the provider is created, before the user has a chance to start editing, so their cursor appears immediately to others.

cards.front: What architectural principle does 'schema = type' express, and which three Meridian tools implement it?
cards.back: **Single source of truth**: define the shape once, derive all representations from it. The three tools: (1) Zod — `z.infer<typeof schema>` derives the TypeScript type from the runtime validator. (2) Drizzle — `$inferSelect`/`$inferInsert` derive TypeScript types from the table definition. (3) `drizzle-zod`'s `createInsertSchema()` — derives a Zod validator from the table definition, linking all three.

cards.front: What would a developer need to do to use Lexical in a Next.js application, and why does this defeat the purpose?
cards.back: They'd need `dynamic(() => import('./LexicalEditor'), { ssr: false })` to disable server-side rendering for the editor component entirely. This means: no SSR for the functional core of the product, initial page load is a blank shell for the editor area, and they've opted out of Next.js's primary feature for the component that matters most. They'd get Next.js's routing and framework overhead without its SSR benefit for the editor.

cards.front: Write the PostgreSQL RLS policy for a `documents` table that enforces workspace isolation using the `SET LOCAL` pattern.
cards.back: `ALTER TABLE documents ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` then `CREATE POLICY workspace_isolation ON documents USING (workspace_id = current_setting('app.current_workspace_id')::uuid);`. The `current_setting()` reads the transaction-scoped variable set by `SET LOCAL app.current_workspace_id = $workspaceId` in the tenant middleware before every query.

cards.front: ProseMirror's `collab` module is mature and used by Notion and many others. Why is it still the wrong choice for Meridian?
cards.back: ProseMirror's collaboration module uses OT (Operational Transformation), which requires a central server to serialize and transform all operations. Yjs uses CRDT, which doesn't. Meridian chose Yjs for offline capability and peer-to-peer correctness — these properties are incompatible with OT's central authority requirement. Using ProseMirror's `collab` with Yjs simultaneously is not supported; they use incompatible operational models.

cards.front: In an Anthropic SDK streaming response, what event type carries the text being generated, and how do you extract the text chunk?
cards.back: Event type: `'content_block_delta'` with `event.delta.type === 'text_delta'`. Extract with: `event.delta.text`. This is the string fragment to send to the SSE client. The switch pattern: `if (event.type === 'content_block_delta' && event.delta.type === 'text_delta') { res.write('data: ' + JSON.stringify({ delta: event.delta.text }) + '\n\n') }`.

cards.front: In a `turbo.json` pipeline, what does `dependsOn: ['^build']` mean, and how does it differ from `dependsOn: ['build']`?
cards.back: `dependsOn: ['^build']` means: before running this package's build, first run `build` in all of this package's workspace *dependencies* (the `^` prefix means 'in dependencies'). `dependsOn: ['build']` (no `^`) means: run `build` in the *same package* first (self-dependency, rarely needed). The caret is essential for ensuring `@meridian/db` builds before `@meridian/server` which depends on it.

cards.front: What is the research's central claim about the optimal learning sequence for Meridian's architecture (C17)?
cards.back: The optimal sequence follows the logical dependency tree of the architecture, not a topic-by-topic survey. TypeScript strict mode and immutability must come first (they gate everything), then the client layer (immutability applied), then the collaboration layer (CRDT theory → Yjs → Lexical in that order), then the data layer, then the AI layer, then the security synthesis. Skipping the foundations makes subsequent layers appear arbitrary.

cards.front: What are the correct SSE response headers for an Express route that streams AI tokens, and why does each matter?
cards.back: `Content-Type: text/event-stream` — tells the browser this is SSE, not JSON. `Cache-Control: no-cache` — prevents proxies and CDNs from buffering the stream. `Connection: keep-alive` — keeps the HTTP connection open for the duration of the stream. Without `no-cache`, a proxy might buffer all tokens and deliver them at once, defeating the streaming UX.

cards.front: What is `startTransition` in React 18, and why does it require immutable state to be safe?
cards.back: `startTransition` marks a state update as non-urgent, allowing React to interrupt rendering it to handle higher-priority updates. When the low-priority render is interrupted and restarted, React may re-read state. If state is mutable and changed between the first read and the re-read, different parts of the interrupted render use different state versions — this is tearing. Immutable state means every re-read returns the same snapshot.

cards.front: What does `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` do to a PostgreSQL table, compared to a table without it?
cards.back: Without RLS: all rows are visible to any role that has SELECT permission. With RLS enabled: no rows are visible by default — a row is only visible if at least one active RLS policy's `USING` clause evaluates to true for that row. The database user used by the application must NOT be a superuser or have `BYPASSRLS` — otherwise RLS is skipped entirely.

cards.front: Why is the `node_modules` volume exclusion pattern (`/app/node_modules`) critical in Docker Compose dev volume mounts?
cards.back: Without it, the host's `node_modules` (installed on the host OS) overwrites the container's `node_modules` (installed in the container's Linux environment). Host modules compiled for Windows/macOS can't run on Linux. The exclusion `- /app/node_modules` tells Docker to keep the container's own `node_modules` directory as a Docker-managed anonymous volume — never overwritten by the host bind mount.
